title: The Gay Bar Is Fine, Actually
summary:
When I asked around for an informal vibe check on the state of the gay bar, I expected the worst. But the sentiment was positively sunny.

The post The Gay Bar Is Fine, Actually appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
The banks aren’t tossing out rainbow pens from a float this year. This is how I typically restock my annual supply, so I’m forced to write this on a laptop. In 2015, the White House was lit up in rainbow colors. Now, a massive UFC arena—the spiritual opposite of a gay light display—has been erected on the lawn. No one drinks or has sex anymore. Support for same-sex marriage has fallen for the first time in decades. 2026 ought to be a horrendous time for the beleaguered institution of the local gay bar. But if you ask me, there’s never been a better time to enjoy a whiskey Coke poured by a bear into a plastic cup at a 5:1 whiskey-to-Coke ratio.

Throughout my long and prodigious career as a homosexual, gays have been complaining about their own bars. Complaining is a great gay artform. During the peak “corporate Pride” years, when Chipotle sold “Homo Estás” shirts, we complained it was pandering; when the sponsors fled after Trump’s reelection, we complained about that, too. Gay bars, we say, are too crowded, too expensive, too exclusionary. The music sucks. The service is awful. The only good bar in this town closed years ago. Do you remember Tic-Tac? It was perfect. It was too good for this wretched world. RIP. And so on. So when I went asking around for an informal vibe check on gay bars in our gloomy present, I expected the worst. The sentiment was positively sunny.

Why were so many gays expressing optimism. Were they sick?

“How’s the gay bar scene here?” I asked a friend while sitting at Trade in Washington, D.C.

“Honestly, great,” he said in an even, earnest tone. “They’re opening new ones,” he went on, despite the face I was making at him.

It was true in New York as well, where bars like Animal regularly see lines wrapped around the block. New York and D.C. are hardly representative of the collective, but I’d recently had the chance, for work, to travel to Denver, Minneapolis, and Houston, and found the gay bar scenes there more vivid than ever.

Vibes Shift, but the Gay Bar Endures

The dimmer the world, the brighter the bar. We trade the danger outside for a danger of our own. 

If you’ll allow a Queer Elder to wax poetic: despite alcohol allegedly being in its flop era, the gay bar has always been something apart. Like wine at Catholic mass, alcohol has an organizing power over it, but the true occasion is metaphysical. The best ones I’ve patronized have been, colloquially speaking, a hole: shabby little dinghies where you can hear the ferocious waves slapping at the sides.

Why the bar? Stonewall is the most obvious example, but the bar remains a vital organ, the health of which is a perennial matter of urgency. Are we letting too many straight people in? Should we start playing porn to keep them out? Or would that be biphobic? Misogynistic? Transphobic? I’ve attended many such symposiums while sipping nail polish remover. I can’t think of another community outside of swingers where the bar has such central importance.

Before even the word “gay,” there was “molly.” And before gay bars, there was something like them: the molly houses of 18th-century London. These were taverns, or coffeehouses, or any place where men could gather to drink and parody the straight world with, for example, mock weddings. The threat of arrest, and even death, hung over these places. It lent them their edge. 

A lot has changed since then. Gay bars are more likely these days to be called “a safe space where you can be your authentic self.” But, in keeping with its molly house roots, I find the gay bar to be something better than that. It’s a slightly dangerous space, where something honest and hungry comes out to play, and to eat. At the gay bar I enter a different self: more mischievous, more open to appetite and suggestion, more liable to leave knowing someone I didn’t know before. There’s a Dionysian aspect. Inebriation leads to revelation. Without danger, there’s no change. Without danger, there’d be no gay bar in the first place.

Change comes slowly or all at once. Tides shift, popularity waxes and wanes, and through it all the drinks have stayed stiff and we have kept complaining. What the future holds for the gay bar, like everything else in our world, is uncertain. Some, like the lesbian bar Henrietta Hudson which rebranded as a “lesbian-centric queer human bar,” have embraced the idea of becoming a “space.” Others, like the beloved Avenue Pub in Rochester did last year, are shuttering. Pride itself is in a precarious place. In the absence of corporate support, the traditional “Pride Is a Riot!” clarion call, too, feels as though it’s lacking its typical annual oomph. There’s a Girl, whatever, hanging like a pall. A sleepy Pride. A languid Pride. A great time to skip the party and hit the bar. Bartender, pour me a vodka cran!

No spoilers, but the tides will change again. Even now, Target has been successfully bullied into an anemic Pride merch collection this year, which includes among its wares a baffling trinket of two felt heterosexual birds driving a car, though one ought not, I suppose, deny the possibility of the birds being bisexual, or transgender. The point is, presidents and vibe shifts come and go. Stiff drinks and gay men complaining are forever. 

“Can we leave before the show starts?” my friend said, sitting across from me at Trade. “I know this queen. She’s not who she thinks she is.”

Ah, it feels good to be home.

title: A Wine Lover’s Guide to Hosting a Fourth of July Party
summary:
Break out the grill, ice bucket, and red, white, and blue decor for an easy, on-theme celebration.

The post A Wine Lover’s Guide to Hosting a Fourth of July Party appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
The best Fourth of July barbecues feel lavish without requiring the host to lay out 14 dishes, monitor the grill like an overworked short-order cook, and spend the evening refilling glasses and sending people out for more ice. 

The trick is to put the effort where guests will notice it most. Choose a strong main dish, be generous with sides, make the drinks self-serve, and use a few decorative flourishes that feel festive without making the entire event look like the seasonal aisle at Hobby Lobby. Add cold, backyard-barbecue-friendly wines, an epic playlist, and the right company, and you may actually get to enjoy the party yourself. 

To help you pull it off (with minimal chaos), we asked chefs, wine pros, and serial party planners for their best tips for hosting a memorable Fourth of July barbecue .

Use the Good Napkins—and the Paper Plates

The whole point of outdoor dining is to keep things as unfussy and mess-free as possible. But that doesn’t mean it has to look haphazardly thrown together either. Lifestyle content creator and home décor expert Lindsay Stewart recommends concentrating on one detail that delivers plenty of impact without creating more work.

“Cloth napkins,” she says. “Don’t waste time and energy on real plates or real flatware. Red, white, and blue cloth napkins are the best bang for your buck and make things feel elevated. Plus, they’re easier to launder than washing a stack of porcelain plates. Paper plates make clean-up a breeze.”

Cover plastic or folding tables with reusable white tablecloths, then use napkins, a simple runner, or bright serving pieces to bring in color. This creates a thoughtful setting without forcing anyone to face a stack of dirty dishes later.

Go All-American with the Wine List

Party themes don’t need to stop at décor and dress codes. In fact, the wine list can carry the thread even further through either distinctly American bottles, or wines with an important place in the country’s history.

Consider serving Schramsberg’s Blanc de Blancs, whose 1969 vintage was poured during President Nixon’s 1972 “Toast to Peace” in Beijing, or Ridge Geyserville, a historic Zinfandel-based red made from a Sonoma site with original vines planted in 1882. For something unexpected, look to Virginia, where Thomas Jefferson’s early wine ambitions still loom large, or North Carolina, once the country’s leading wine-producing state before Prohibition. A bottle from Biltmore Estate Winery offers a modern taste of that Blue Ridge wine legacy. 

Don’t forget Madeira. Though Portuguese, the fortified wine was deeply woven into colonial American life and is said to have been used to toast the signing of the Declaration of Independence. Plus, it makes a killer pairing with a round of s’mores.

Give Barbecue the Red Sparkler It Deserves

Barbecue can be a pairing minefield if you’re not intentional about the drinks selection. You’ve got smoke, sweet sauce, spice, char, salty snacks, and creamy sides—sometimes mixing and mingling on the same deliciously chaotic plate.

Rosé is a flexible starting point, with a kaleidoscope of styles, from light and dry Provençal bottles to brambly and fruity Spanish sippers. Chillable reds such as Gamay or Frappato work well in a warm-weather setting and with varied flavors. For something a bit more unexpected but sure to sing with backyard barbecue fare, Kojanis recommends Lambrusco.

“Its combination of dark fruit, lively acidity, and gentle sparkle makes it incredibly good with burgers, ribs, sausages, and smoky barbecue sauces,” he says. “And don’t overlook sparkling wine in general—the bubbles help cut through richness and cleanse the palate between bites.”

Focus on One Protein, but Lots of Toppings

A great Fourth of July barbecue does not require five different proteins and a host frantically trying to manage all of them.

Choosing just one protein to grill allows you to invest your time and energy into toppings, which are generally much easier to prep ahead (or require no prep at all.) Former chef and home cook Kendell Styles uses that strategy for a build-your-own chili dog bar. “I make my chili the day before and prep all the toppings,” he says. “Then, when it’s party time, I warm the chili and buns, set out the toppings, open a few bags of chips, and put out a few side salads, and I get to enjoy the party with everyone else.” 

Styles also prepares pasta, macaroni, and potato salads the day before, noting that they often taste better the next day. Keep corn chips on hand to give leftover chili a second life as Frito pie when the evening turns to after-hours.

The same approach works for burgers. Hale keeps the beef patties simple, then adds one signature topping, like caramelized onions, bacon jam, or pimento cheese. A compact sauce bar with barbecue sauce, chimichurri, pickled onions, spicy aioli, and fresh slaw lets guests completely customize the same main. Plenty of these condiments can be store-bought or made ahead, depending on how ambitious a home cook you are.

Use Both Sides of Your Grill (and Don’t Forget to Preheat)

You don’t need a Big Green Egg, a secret family recipe, or lineage in one of America’s great barbecue regions to turn out excellent food from the grill. Some of the best tips are actually the most straightforward and can level up your grill game in a matter of minutes.

“Use a two-zone fire,” urges Hale. “It’s one of the simplest techniques professional chefs use that translates perfectly to a backyard barbecue.”

On a charcoal grill, bank the coals to one side. On a gas grill, keep one side hot and the other cooler, or turn it off completely. Use the hot zone to get the perfect sear, then move thicker cuts to the cooler side to finish or stay warm without turning into jerky. “It’s like having two cooking appliances in one,” says Hale.

When it comes to grilling, Styles also issues an important PSA: don’t forget to preheat your grill. “You have to be sure the grill is hot so food doesn’t stick and you can serve guests quickly,” he says.

Lastly, don’t shy away from ribs. They’re actually far easier than their reputation suggests. Styles cooks his in the oven or pressure cooker until they’re tender, and then finishes them on the grill for that signature char. There are plenty of worthy store-bought rubs and sauces out there to make it look like you spent some formative years in Memphis.

Decorate with Patterns, Not Just Flags

The goal is to suggest the Fourth of July without the entire backyard looking like a fireworks stand. 

Stewart recommends mixing solid red, white, and blue pieces with checks, stripes, and polka dots, rather than covering everything in flag motifs. “You get the variation with prints and things still feel festive without being too obvious,” she says. “Plus, you’ll find that you can reuse all of this and you won’t get tired of an overt theme across all of your linens.”

A white tablecloth and a festive runner can anchor the table. Add grocery store or farmers’ market flowers, pillar candles in hurricanes, and small cotton flags tucked into urns or flower pots. Red, white, and blue bunting along a fence, railing, or buffet table can create a standout backdrop and can be folded away for next year.

Build a Self-Serve Bar That Actually Serves Itself

A drinks station is only self-serve if guests can figure it out without tracking down the host. 

Stewart prints or handwrites a small menu of available drinks, places it in a frame, and keeps everything—wine, ice, glasses, openers, and garnishes—in one central spot. 

“An ice bucket with tongs is a must, plus a large galvanized tub with ice and a variety of white, rosé, sparkling wine, and water,” she says. “[Also put out] a dish and tongs with garnishes like sliced lemons and limes and a big cooler with bags of ice for backup.”

Open the wine in advance and keep ice nearby. Water should be just as visible as the alcohol—remember, this is a marathon, not a sprint. Put out pitchers or dispensers, perhaps one with lemon and another with cucumber, and designate someone as the water monitor.

“Water, water, water,” Stewart says. “You’ll never regret having water in pitchers or in decanters with spigots.”

A setup that answers guests’ questions before they ask them does more than improve the flow of the party. It allows the host to actually attend it.

title: One of the Best Parts of Bars Is Disappearing Without a Sound
summary:
Analog jukeboxes are now as rare as $8 drink specials. This quiet shift has thunderous implications.

The post One of the Best Parts of Bars Is Disappearing Without a Sound appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Go to a bar. Close your eyes and listen. Amid the din of people clinking glasses and sharing alarmingly personal anecdotes with their bartenders, you probably won’t hear a once-ubiquitous sound: the clatter of someone flipping through the pages of an old-school jukebox.

That’s because analog jukeboxes—the kind with curated albums you might need quarters or smooth bills to play—are now as rare as $8 drink specials. Over the last 15 years, many were replaced by digital jukeboxes, backed by private equity funding and limitless cloud-based music libraries.

This quiet shift has thunderous implications. Jukeboxes with hand-picked soundtracks give bars their singular character and invite anyone within earshot to engage with their surroundings. It’s all part of what makes bars more than just places to drink. The best ones become third spaces whose distinctive personalities build connections and community. In those bars, everybody might not know your name, but they’ll certainly listen to your song.

The primary digital jukebox disruptor is TouchTunes, an international corporation with machines in 65,000 venues. Their business is booming. Analysts estimate that the market for digital jukeboxes will grow by nearly 9% to $812 million by 2033. (Meanwhile, pre-owned old-school Wurlitzer jukeboxes sell for $220 or best offer on eBay.)

In 2010, TouchTunes introduced an app that lets users control the soundtrack in public spaces without looking up from their phones. Rather than empowering individualism, it resulted in seas of sameness: According to TouchTunes’ data, mainstream chart-toppers headline their most popular playlists.

On the other hand, the idiosyncratic assortment of tracks in an old-school jukebox expresses a point of view. That specificity creates a sense of belonging, explains Jesse Rifkin, owner of Walk on the Wild Side Tours and author of This Must Be The Place: Music, Community and Vanished Spaces in New York City. “There’s been very deliberate thinking about what kind of environment whoever programmed that jukebox is trying to create,” he says. “When you mix all the colors together, it becomes gray.”

There’s also a tall order at hand that might just come with long-term benefits: You have to get off your phone and barstool to play an analog jukebox. You might even strike up a conversation with someone while you wait to play your song. It “makes the act of passively listening to music in a public setting interactive,” Rifkin says.

If you’re shy or find strangers generally unpleasant, this may sound unappealing. But low-stakes interactions are excellent for our well-being. A study of more than 66,000 people found correlations between cognitive function and social engagement, according to Harvard Health Publishing. As record numbers of Americans self-identify as lonely, and hospitality businesses navigate epic headwinds, this feels poignant.

Reinstalling old-school jukeboxes won’t cure social isolation or singlehandedly revive the bar industry. But I believe that digitally powerwashing the character out of third spaces makes them less inviting when we need them—and they need us— the most.

Besides, if there’s a better use for a fistful of quarters than reveling in Wu-Tang throwbacks or Bonnie Raitt B-sides that some grizzled bartender felt was best for their bar’s vibe, I’m all ears.

title: Strawberries and Black Tea Join Forces in This Bittersweet Campari Cocktail
summary:
With Campari, strawberries, and fresh citrus, Roman Hills puts a tart Italian twist on the classic cobbler.

The post Strawberries and Black Tea Join Forces in This Bittersweet Campari Cocktail appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
“We’re trying to evoke the fresh, green pastures of Rome,” says Kay Baldasare, bar manager of New York City Italian restaurant Ci Siamo.

The Roman Hills cocktail, a collaboration between Baldasare and Chef Hillary Sterling, is a fruit-packed refresher designed to accompany rich cheeses, pastas, and wood-fired vegetable dishes. “The strawberries are a natural fit for all the savory things on the menu,” Baldasare explains, adding that their natural sweetness is balanced by the tannins and bitterness in the Campari and black tea.

The drink riffs on the classic cobbler, one of the oldest cocktail templates, dating back to the early 1800s. Typically, a cobbler mixes a spirit with sugar, seasonal fruit, and an herb. Here, the herbal component is represented by Campari, and while citrus isn’t part of the historic version, fresh lemon and lime add a welcome acidic counterpoint.

The original drink was built over “cobbles” of ice, resembling the small stones used to pave cobblestone streets, giving the drink its name. Today, the drink is more likely to be made with crushed ice rather than pebble ice.

Roman Hills

Kay Baldasare, Bar Manager, Ci Siamo, NYC

Ingredients

3 strawberry halves
1 ounce vodka
1 ounce Campari
3⁄4 ounce black tea syrup*
1⁄2 ounce lemon juice
1⁄2 ounce lime juice
Mint sprig, for garnish

Directions

In a mixing tin, use a muddler or the back of a wooden spoon to crush the strawberry halves and extract the juice. Add remaining ingredients and ice. Shake well, then double strain into a Collins glass over crushed ice.

*To make black tea syrup: Make simple syrup by mixing equal parts sugar and hot water, stirring until sugar dissolves.

Dip two black tea bags (such as English breakfast) into hot water. Quickly squeeze out excess water, then steep the tea bags in ¼ cup simple syrup for 1 hour. Remove the tea bags, and squeeze excess syrup back into the cup (to avoid sticky fingers, place the bag on a spoon, then wrap the tea bag string around the bag and bowl of the spoon). Discard tea bags. Makes enough for 2 drinks, plus a little extra.

title: How Wine and Spirits Got Stuck in the Middle of a Global Trade War
summary:
An Exhaustive (and Exhausting) Timeline of the American Tariff Roller Coaster Over the last eight years, the alcohol industry has faced chaos and cost increases at the hands of a global trade war. Here’s where we currently stand and how we got here. By Kate Dingwall Ever since President Trump’s first round in the Oval

The post How Wine and Spirits Got Stuck in the Middle of a Global Trade War appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
An Exhaustive (and Exhausting) Timeline of the American Tariff Roller Coaster

Over the last eight years, the alcohol industry has faced chaos and cost increases at the hands of a global trade war. Here’s where we currently stand and how we got here.

By Kate Dingwall

Ever since President Trump’s first round in the Oval Office, the back-and-forth of various trade wars has made headlines. All throughout, the beverage and alcohol industry has been firmly lodged in the center of the conflict.

The first blow came in 2018. Triggered by a breakdown of talks over steel and aluminum, the European Union imposed a retaliatory tariff on American whiskey. In response, the U.S. government, led by President Trump, announced a series of sky-high retaliatory tariffs, many of which take aim at U.K.– and E.U.-produced alcohol.

Champagne producers, whisk(e)y distillers, restaurateurs, and importers on both sides of the Atlantic are still feeling the repercussions.

Despite the widespread damage, the tariff conversation came back around with Trump’s third run for president. As soon as he returned to the Oval Office in early 2025, he turned up the heat, with official announcements declaring 25%, 50%, and 200% tariffs.

Through this all, Scotch producers, Champagne makers, Bourbon distillers, Mexican tequileros, and the importers, sellers, and drinkers of all of the above have been subjected to an ongoing period of negotiating, pivoting, worrying, and navigating chaos.

In February, nearly a year after the first lawsuits were filed, the Supreme Court deemed the tariffs illegal. Importers immediately began suing the U.S. government to return the approximate $175 billion it collected. To help unpack where we currently stand and may go from here, we laid out the complete chronology of the current rollercoaster of global tariffs (along with some historical context) and how it affects the wine and spirits industry.

1791

Tariffs first hit the American alcohol industry after the Revolutionary War, with a levy on all imported and American-made distilled spirits to help pay off the national debt.

The federal government initiated similar tariffs after the Civil War—when up to 40% of the government’s income came from alcohol taxes—during Prohibition, the Great Depression, and World War II to help the country rebuild.

1994

The North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA) dictates that American, Mexican, and Canadian wine and spirits can criss-cross the border without tax implications.

1997

A similar trade agreement is installed between the U.S. and E.U. With it, American whiskey and wine and European alcoholic beverages can move across the ocean sans levies.

June 2018

Under President Donald Trump’s first term, a dispute over aluminum and steel results in a broad-hitting tariff war. In response to American tariffs on steel and aluminum, the E.U. imposes a 25% retaliatory tariff on American whiskey. Mexico implements a 25% tariff on U.S.-made whiskey, too.

October 2019

Another conflict arises. Longstanding tensions surrounding Boeing and Airbus subsidies result in a new wave of transatlantic tariffs.

The U.S. rolls out a 25% retaliatory duty specifically on French wines, Scottish single malts, Irish whiskey, and Italian cheeses. Also hit: still wines from Germany, Spain, France, and Britain with under 14% alcohol by volume, such as Chablis and Alsatian Rieslings, and liqueurs and cordials from Germany, Ireland, Italy, Spain, and the U.K.

November 2020

As the aerospace manufacturing dispute continues, the European Union includes American rums, brandies, and vodkas under its 25% tariff umbrella.

June 2021

Six months after President Trump leaves office for the first time, the U.S., U.K., and E.U. agree to suspend tariffs related to the WTO Boeing-Airbus disputes, for five years.

Over the next two years, under President Biden, all tariffs are settled. The E.U. suspends retaliatory tariffs on American whiskey in March of 2022, while the U.K. removes its tariffs on the category in June of 2022.

While the tariff crisis may be over—for now—the ramifications are still sending shockwaves through the industry. Over the three-year period, starting in 2018, American whiskey exports drop 42%, while Scotch whisky imports are down by almost 38%.

2024

During his presidential campaign Donald Trump pledges tariffs bigger than ever before. Threats include 100% tariffs on Mexico, 60% on China, and 20% on all other countries.

He admits the tariffs may cause “some pain” for Americans, but adds, “it will all be worth the price that must be paid.”

January 2025

President Trump is sworn into office on January 20. He pledges he will instate a 25% tariff on Canada and Mexico, including alcoholic beverages like whisky and tequila, starting February 1.

Even if alcohol is excluded from final rates, industry insiders are freaking out. Corks, casks, glass, bottle stoppers, concrete eggs, and countless other production essentials are sourced from the global supply chain. If the costs of these products increase, so will the cost of doing business.

February 2025

On February 1, President Trump signs an executive order announcing 25% tariffs on Mexico and Canada to commence February 4. He claims it is a national emergency triggered by drug trafficking and undocumented immigration between the three nations.

Two days later, on February 3, Trump agrees to a 30-day pause. The industry breathes a momentary sigh of relief.

February 13 rolls around: President Trump restarts the tariff conversation, announcing he will increase U.S. tariffs to match other international tax rates. Called the “Fair and Reciprocal Plan,” the administration claims it’s about fairness this time.

March 2025

The 30-day pause ends on March 4. Trump’s 25% tariffs are set into motion for Canada and Mexico. After throwing around varied rates—from 10% to 145%—China’s are set at 20%.

Anger spreads throughout all three nations. Mexico’s President Claudia Sheinbaum promises retaliatory tariffs, as does Canada’s Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.

Influenced by higher-ups at automotive manufacturers Ford, General Motors, and Stellantis, Trump grants a one-month exemption for all imports from Canada and Mexico to help U.S. automakers. This time, reciprocal tariffs are slated to go in motion on April 2.

That’s not good enough for the European Union. They retaliate on March 12, announcing new duties on U.S. farm and industrial products, including bourbon and wine. Canada also takes retaliatory trade action—imposing retaliatory tariffs worth $20 billion on U.S. imports including wine, spirits, and beer.

Canadian citizens, business, and local governments also boycott American products. Media reports circulate videos of Canadians pulling Kentucky bourbon off liquor store shelves.

President Trump responds in force. On March 13, he threatens a 200% tariff on European Champagne, wine, and spirits. In response, the E.U. moves to hit American whiskey with a 50% tariff.

The U.S. Wine Trade Alliance urges American companies to pause shipments of European wine, spirits, and beer, or face potential 200% tariffs.

“This would be a death knell for our company and our industry,” says Harmon Skurnik, president of Skurnik Wine & Spirits.

This red light, green light game of tariffs continues until early September.

April 2025

The Trump administration declares April 2 to be “Liberation Day.” With baited breath, the wine and spirits industries wait for the scheduled press conference announcing upcoming tariffs.

It’s less dramatic than expected. Trump announces a 10% baseline tax on imports from all countries, with more specific levies on certain countries. China faces a 34% tariff; the E.U., 20%; South Africa, 31%;, and Japan, 24%.

On April 9, Trump authorizes a 90-day pause on most tariffs to allow time for deliberation. It’s a relief, albeit a temporary one, for producers and importers.

May 2025

On May 26, Trump announces he’s delaying the 50% tariff on the E.U. until July 9 to give both sides more time to negotiate.

Beverage industry organizations, including the Toasts Not Tariffs Coalition, rally for tariff-free trade for wine and spirits. They’re optimistic, hoping for zero-for-zero tariffs. If the proposed tariffs were to come to life, the entire beverage industry could be collateral damage.

As beverage experts point out, it’s already a dire time for wine and spirits.

“The industry is facing tremendous headwinds of late,” says Brett Dunne, managing director U.S. and Canada of Lucas Bols. “It’s due to multiple factors, such as the declining consumption habits of younger generations, the emergence of THC beverages and recreational cannabis use, the use of GLP1 drugs, and the overall impact the economy has had on consumer goods. And as a whole, inflation continues to remain relatively high.”

August 2025

It’s official: on August 7, new tariffs take effect. The European Union, New Zealand, and Japan are subject to 15% tax, and 30% for South Africa. Canada is slapped with a 35% tariff rate, though this does not apply to alcoholic beverages.

Industry professionals are disappointed.

“This is an unsteady time for the U.S. hospitality industry with sales of spirits facing a slowdown and rising food and labor costs,” says Distilled Spirits Council President and CEO Chris Swonger. “These new higher tariffs on E.U. spirits products will further compound the challenges facing restaurants and bars nationwide.”

September 2025

Winemakers, importers, restaurant owners, and spirits producers are devastated. Though far less than what was originally threatened, even 15% is catastrophic news for the bottom line.

Despite all the back-and-forth, these newly implemented tariffs aren’t finite. In early September, the U.S. Court of Appeals rules that most of the Trump administration’s tariffs are “invalid as contrary to law”—i.e. illegal.

They are left in place, as the case moves toward the Supreme Court for further deliberations.

November 2025

The Supreme Court questions the legality of Trump’s tariffs, and considers striking them down.

At the center of the debate: V.O.S. Selections, a New York City-based wine importer who is challenging Trump’s imposition of tariffs in the case of Trump v. V.O.S. Selections. The company argues the administration violated a federal law, one that only allows Trump to interfere with a foreign country’s property (i.e. wine and its importing) in the case of “an unusual and extraordinary threat.” V.O.S. Selects argues that no unusual and extraordinary threat has occurred.

Countries with tariffs in place wait with bated breath.

If the tariffs are overturned, the Trump administration would have to repay the billions of dollars in tariffs the U.S. has collected this year. Trump’s tariffs have generated over $100 billion in tax revenue to date.

Mid-month, the Commerce Department releases data showing the toll tariffs have taken on trade, as of August 7. (The report was delayed seven weeks due to government shutdowns.) With global shoppers purchasing fewer American products due to boycotts and other factors, U.S. exports fell $500 million in August. Imports dropped 5.1%.

The White House announces more than 200 food products will have their tariffs removed, including coffee, tea, fruit juice, and tomatoes as Americans express frustration over the growing cost of groceries. Wine is not included in the list.

December 2025

Seven months after multiple Canadian provinces banned American wine and spirits (which collapsed exports to Canada by 85%), over $100 million worth of U.S. products are still stockpiled in the warehouses. 

As a result some provinces are releasing US-made bottles to the public again—with some caveats. The province of Quebec is donating soon-to-expire American liquor to charitable events as well as schools that focus on hospitality and catering. Prince Edward Island is also donating the net profits of its American alcohol sales to food banks. And Nova Scotia started selling off its stores on December 1, but officials say the province will not be ordering any more after the inventory is gone.

American producers are feeling the loss of what was once a major market. Wineries are struggling with the loss of the United States’ top export market and major spirits producers are cutting back. On December 21, Jim Beam announced it would pause production at a major plant, with analysts citing Trump’s tariffs as the root of the problem.

February 2026

In a 6 to 3 decision, the U.S. Supreme Court strikes down Trump’s tariffs. They’re illegal, the court deems. President Trump exceeded his authority.The now-vetoed tariffs covered imported goods from more than 100 countries, including wine and spirits from France, Italy, South Africa, and beyond.

“It’s a fantastic day for American justice when a small company like ours, with strong ideals and backbone, can have their case heard at the U.S. Supreme Court and win,” Vos Selections, the small American wine importer who was the lead plaintiff on the case, posted on Instagram.

But there are unanswered questions with the decision. The Supreme Court does not address if, how, and when potential refunds of over $175 billion would be issued to the importers who paid the illegal tariffs. Justice Kavanaugh warns that the process will be “a mess,” and a major blow to President Trump’s agenda and the federal budget. Importing companies, including Fedex, begin suing the U.S. government for refunds.

Trump calls the Supreme Court justices “fools and lap dogs,” finds a workaround, and sets a blanket tariff of 10% starting Tuesday, February 24th. Trump warns a further 15% will “come later.”

April 2026

As of 8 am on April 20th, importers, brokers, and other businesses who paid the Trump administration’s tariffs can file for refunds with the U.S. Customs and Border Protection. The agency currently expects approved claims to be paid within 90 days, though the government notes it will focus on recent tariff payments first. 

Many are pessimistic—a lot of money needs to move through a newly-made claims system in a relatively short period of time. In court filings, Customs and Border Protection flagged that over 330,000 importers are owed $166 billion on some 53 million shipments. Currently, over 50,000 importers eligible for refunds totalling $127 billion have filed for refunds.

May 2026

Following King Charles’ visit to the White House in late April, Donald Trump announces the 10% baseline tariff will be removed on Scotch and Irish whiskey, and restrictions will be loosened on Scotland’s ability to work with Kentucky on whisky and bourbon. “The King and Queen got me to do something that nobody else was able to do, without hardly even asking,” Trump said on Truth Social.

July 2026

While the tariff refund process is relatively painless—importers are receiving reimbursements—Trump’s position on Scotch and Irish Whiskey from April isn’t translating to other nations. In response to France’s digital tax—a 3% levy on large U.S. tech companies, including Amazon and Meta—Trump threatens a 100% tariff on French wine.

Meanwhile, the U.S. government continues to try and appeal in the Court of International Trade on the tariff refund order.

title: 9 of the Largest Wine Collections in the World
summary:
You might have a sizable wine collection, but it’s a speck in the dust compared to these extensive cellars.

The post 9 of the Largest Wine Collections in the World appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Whoever said there can be too much of a good thing surely didn’t consider one exception: the wine cellar.

Some of the world’s newest and most historic properties boast impressive cellars that are outdone only by the wines themselves, like 2000 Pétrus and 1845 Château Gruaud Larose.

The only thing as good as being able to choose from these immense inventories, of course, is the chance to see them firsthand. Luckily, these venues showcase their best. From a $10 million spectacle in Wyoming to a subterranean city in Moldova, here are some of the globe’s most groundbreaking wine collections worthy of a visit.

Milestii Mici: 2,000,000 bottles

Founded in 1969, Melestii Mici is a Moldovan producer whose esteem is further heightened by its epic wine collection. Recognized in 2005 by the Guinness Book of World Records as the world’s largest cellar by number of bottles, it now holds over two millions bottles. A cellar this size needs room to breathe. Visitors explore the 34-mile gallery via car, organized by “streets” named for each wine.

Hôtel de Paris Monte-Carlo: 300,000 bottles

Inspired by the grand cellars of Bordeaux, this space was built in 1874 behind Monte Carlo’s famed Hôtel de Paris. Once completed, the cellar’s combined surface area reached more than 16,000 square feet. Guests can taste through the selection at either the onsite, Michelin-starred restaurants, or a private dining experience in the cellars. Monaco’s Prince Rainier and Princess Grace celebrated their 20th anniversary there.

Bern’s Steakhouse: 500,000 bottles

Bern’s Steakhouse, a member of Wine Enthusiast‘s Restaurant Hall of Fame, opened in 1956 and has a loyal following for its lauded wine collection. The focus at the Tampa restaurant is on France and California, and the cellar includes rarities like an 1845 Château Gruaud Larose. Diners can check out more than 150 options by the glass to pair with the restaurant’s prized plates, from Chateaubriand carpaccio and duck confit to Osetra caviar and foie gras a la plancha.

The Graycliff: 250,000 bottles

Located in a historic 18th-century mansion, this lavish hotel in Nassau, Bahamas, houses the Caribbean’s first five-star restaurant, an accolade enhanced only by its colossal wine collection. Owner Enrico Garzaroli has curated the selection for decades. It showcases more than 5,000 wineries from 20 countries. Rarities include an 1865 Château Lafite and a 1727 Rüdesheimer Apostelwein.

Marqués de Riscal’s “Cathedral”: 130,000 bottles

Between a luxury hotel, spa, and Michelin-starred dining, there’s ample reason to visit the Marqués de Riscal City of Wine, one of the oldest and largest wineries in Spain’s Rioja region. Founded in 1858, the winery sprawls across three buildings, and includes the original Bodega 1860 cellar. Guests can view its “Cathedral,” a private collection of bottles from every vintage since the winery’s first 1862 harvest—just a sample of its full inventory.

Restaurant Latour: 100,000 bottles

Situated on the four-star Crystal Springs Resort, this New Jersey restaurant (also a Wine Enthusiast Restaurant Hall of Fame honoree) gained a reputation over the past 15 years for its seasonal New American cuisine. The biggest draw, though, is its wine cellar, a nine-room maze with more than 6,000 labels that focuses on France, Italy, and California. Guests can tour the inventory daily, or book a private exploration of the Bordeaux or Grand Cru rooms.

Brush Creek Ranch: 30,000 bottles

This 30,000-acre Wyoming cattle ranch recently debuted The Farm, a food-and-drink dimension with a distillery, brewery, restaurant, and state-of-the-art cellar. Gretchen Allen, the head sommelier, hopes to grow the subterranean space to 100,000 bottles. It’s an ode to Bordeaux wines, as evidenced by one of the most extensive collections of First Growth wines from all five houses, along with an array of covetable large-format bottlings.

Don Alfonso 1890: 25,000 bottles

This hotel may be located in an Italian town with a population of just 3,000, but its wine collection is larger than life. More than 1,300 labels are offered here, with a particular affinity shown for the wines of Campania, Piedmont, and Tuscany. It’s also known to have one of the largest stashes of Brunello di Montalcino, perfect to pair with the on-site Michelin-starred restaurant’s specialty dish: sucking pig with sweet-and-sour tamarind.

Antoine’s: 6,800 bottles

Antoine Alciatore opened this New Orleans restaurant in 1840, where he helped lay the groundwork for French Creole cuisine. While the venue’s original wine collection was lost during Hurricane Katrina, the team has built it up again in staggering ways. It’s housed in a 165-foot wine “alley” with an emphasis on French finds.

title: What, Exactly, Is ‘Solera’?
summary:
The solera system continually blends younger wines into older ones, so that generations of wine gradually merge and pass their character from one to the next.

The post What, Exactly, Is ‘Solera’? appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Wine folks, particularly collectors, tend to put a lot of value on vintages… and for the most part rightfully so. Some years are wet and cool, while others can be dry and insanely hot. And of course, the conditions change based on country, region, and vineyard. But there is a corner of the wine world, centered largely in the historic bodegas of Jerez, Spain, where specific vintages mean far less than the sum of their parts.

Enter the solera system. It is arguably the most complex, historic, and potentially confusing way to age wine. Instead of bottling wine from a single vintage each year, winemakers blend wines of different ages continuously. Using a technique commonly known as “fractional blending,” the goal isn’t to capture all that the individual year had to offer—instead, it’s to create a consistent, recognizable house style that tastes the same today as it did when your great grandparents shared a bottle over dinner.

Building Wine from Different Barrels

To visualize a solera, imagine a wall of oak barrels stacked in tiers. The bottom row is actually called the solera, derived from the Spanish word for floor. This row contains the oldest wine in the system and is where the final wine destined for the year’s bottles is pulled from. Above it are layers of younger wines, known as criaderas. The first criadera sits directly above the floor barrels, with the second above that, and so on. Some systems are small and may have only two to three layers, while others are massive labyrinths of wood barrels that have been blending wines for decades.

The magic happens through a process of extraction and replenishment, often called “running the scales.” When it’s time to bottle the wine, the winemaker draws a portion from the bottom solera row, but here’s the catch: they never empty the barrels. Usually, they take no more than a third of the liquid from the bottom row.

Once the oldest wine is removed, the barrels have a gap that needs filling. This replenishment is called rocío, or dewing/sprinkling. The winemaker takes wine from the first criadera, the next oldest layer, and moves it down to fill up the bottom solera row. Then, the second criadera tops off the first criadera row, and the third tops up the second. This cascading refill continues until you reach the very top tier of barrels, which is finally filled with the youngest wine from the most recent harvest.

No Bad Years

With this constant movement between various vintages, there’s always a degree of barrel age. Because you’re only moving a small fraction of the wine at a time, the oldest wine remains the dominant soul of the blend. Think of it as Mr. Miyagi training Daniel, imparting his wisdom on the young and arrogant newbie that is full of potential. It’s a process that ensures the final product maintains a reliable, recognizable style. You don’t have to worry about a “bad year” ruining the flavor profile because the sheer volume of older, consistent wine in the system buffers just about all vintage variation. Plus, if there is a poor vintage the amount of wine from said vintage can be limited to how much is added to the top row.

This is also why you rarely see a specific year printed on a bottle of high-quality Sherry, where this process is most utilized. Instead of a vintage, these wines are defined by an average age. When you sip a wine from a solera that has been running since 1920, you’re technically drinking a blend that contains a tiny, microscopic trace of every single vintage harvested since the system began. That’s what makes these solera-aged wines so charming and charismatic.

The environment inside those barrels is just as important as the movement of the liquid. Unlike most table wines, which are kept in topped-up barrels to hide from oxygen, solera barrels are often only partially filled. This leaves a headspace of oxygen that allows the wine to evolve in one of two distinct, and quite different, directions.

The first is biological aging. This is where a specialized layer of yeast called flor forms on the surface of the wine. This living yeast cover protects the wine from oxygen and is used in Sherry styles like Fino or Manzanilla. These wines stay pale, lean, and incredibly fresh with high acidity  and flavors of green apple and almond. The constant addition of new wine into the top tiers is vital here because it provides fresh nutrients the yeast needs to stay alive and the flor needs to stay intact.

The second path is oxidative aging. If the winemaker decides to fortify the wine, the heightened alcohol levels will kill the yeast, thereby halting fermentation on the spot. Without that protective layer of flor, the wine is exposed to oxygen in the barrel. Over years in the solera, the wine turns deep amber or brown, developing intense characteristics of walnuts, dried figs/dates, and all kinds of spice. 

These wines fall under the Oloroso category, where the texture becomes richer, the structure is more pronounced, and the flavors are more concentrated through slow evaporation. Other forms of Sherry, like Amontillado (which starts with biological aging, then switches to oxidative) and Palo Cortado also utilize this path.

Beyond Sherry: Solera in Other Wine Regions

The solera process is used in other parts of the wine world outside of just Sherry production in Jerez. These regions include Sicily (Marsala), Australia (Rutherglen Muscat), Portugal (Madeira), and France (in Banyuls and Champagne). 

Champagne is a tricky one, however, as many houses use multi-vintage blending for their non-vintage (NV) house style blend, which differs slightly from the traditional solera system used for various fortified wines. To maintain a consistent style, Champagne producers blend each new vintage with carefully selected reserve wines from previous years.

This process is absolutely as labor intensive and tedious as it sounds, but it’s worth its weight in gold: These bottles possess a level of complexity that a single-vintage wine simply cannot achieve on its own. 

On the other hand, in fortified wines, a solera system continually introduces younger wines into older ones, so that generations of wine gradually blend together and pass their character from one to the next. The technique balances the brightness and acidity of youth alongside the leathery, savory depth of old age. It is a complex, and when done right, flat out delicious infinite loop of flavor.

So, the next time you see a bottle of Sherry, another solera-aged fortified wine, or even your favorite Champagne, don’t be turned off just because there’s no vintage on the bottle. Know that what’s inside is a blend of history and chemistry, a wine that has quite literally traveled through the ages one barrel at a time, to get to your glass.

title: You Don’t Need to Smoke Cigars to Fall in Love with Cigar Whiskey
summary:
Originally meant to be enjoyed alongside a cigar, these top-rated whiskeys are aged in a blend of Armagnac, Sherry, and Cognac casks.

The post You Don’t Need to Smoke Cigars to Fall in Love with Cigar Whiskey appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Keep your stogies; they’re not for me. While I’ve never warmed to cigars, I was surprised to find how much I enjoy “cigar whiskeys”—a growing category of silky, mellow whiskeys to savor alongside the slow ritual of smoking a cigar.

“The history of cigar blends started in the Cognac world,” explains Nancy Fraley, master blender for Joseph Magnus, where she created the first “cigar blend bourbon” in 2016. While the pairing likely dates back centuries—who knows when the French first thought to pair their signature grape brandy with an indulgent puff?—the modern era kicked off in the 1990s, Fraley says. She points to the debut of Frapin Cognac’s Cigar Blend XO, as well as Old Havana, a “cigar blend brandy” created by American brandy-maker Hubert Germain-Robin.

“That’s where I got my original inspiration,” Fraley says.

What Is a Cigar Whiskey?

In theory, any whiskey sipped alongside a cigar could be labeled as a “cigar whiskey”—it’s not an official category like wheated bourbon or American single malts.

Yet, it has some distinguishing characteristics. For example, one of the qualities of a cigar brandy, Fraley says, is production and aging methods that create rancio, or a flavor profile full of “dark dried fruit, nutty qualities, oxidized esters, fatty acids that tend to pair very well with cigars.” 

So when she built her Magnus Cigar Blend, she carried that over, finishing her whiskey in Armagnac, Sherry, and Cognac casks, “in order to get that sort of rancio note that I can’t get from whiskey just by itself.” Tinged with prune, dried cherry, and clove, it’s an enticing sip—that complexity and full-bodied texture is exactly what I like about these bottles.

From a cigar-smoker’s standpoint, that silky, unctuous mouthfeel serves a purpose, explains Paul Pearse, owner of The Ariston, a cigar bar in Nashville, Tennessee. “One of the complaints of casual cigar smokers is they don’t want to taste it in the morning. Great cigars ‘dissolve.’” When paired harmoniously, “whiskey can help create a coating effect on your palate, where you don’t taste the cigar as long,” he says. “The finish will be held by the whiskey.”

If my tasting notes include hints of tobacco, cigar smoke, or cigar wrapper, that’s likely on me: everyone I spoke with insisted that cigar whiskeys are meant to pair with—not taste like—cigars.

Not Just Cigar Malts

The OG in the cigar whiskey category is Scotch-maker Dalmore. Their influential Dalmore Cigar Malt was introduced in 1999 and discontinued ten years later, only to be revived in 2011 as Dalmore Cigar Malt Reserve: a luscious, toffee-rich sipper I’ve long appreciated, but had thought of as a singular dram, not a category. 

It’s far from the only cigar malt (see also Tomintoul, Tamdhu), but it’s definitely the best-known in the U.S. It’s also the top-seller at Ariston. “We go through a bottle every month or two, pretty consistently,” Pearse estimates.

But the cigar whiskey category is more than just malt. Malt signals malted barley, the primary ingredient in Scotch, as well as American single malts. Among the latter, Virginia Distillery’s new “First Cut,” bursting with red berry and dark chocolate, might give Scotland a run for their money.

I’m also seeing plenty of cigar bourbons and ryes. Barrell Bourbon’s recently-released “cigar blend” of cask-strength bourbons is a shining example, redolent with molasses and dry spices. Interestingly, both First Cut’s and Barrell’s cigar blend include a measure of whiskey finished in Armagnac, a nod to Fraley’s design.

Elsewhere, Kentucky’s Buzzard’s Roost rolled out a duo of cigar bourbons and ryes, finished in heavily-toasted, lightly-charred barrels to create vanilla, “tobacco sweetness,” and “very deep spice” tones, for a “sweet but smoky contrast,” explains Jason Brauner, co-founder and master blender.

What’s Next (and What’s Not)

For now, I’m looking forward to seeing where this smoky path leads. One specific direction is a hard pass for me: “Cigarette blend bourbon,” apparently intended as a cheeky suggestion of the interplay of tobacco, leather, and smoke. (That said, Armagnac, Sherry, and Cognac are part of the finishing mix, so maybe it’s not a complete gimmick?) 

Another I’m keeping on the radar: a “Blunt Blend” rye devised—and trademarked—by Dark Arts Whiskey House in Lexington, Kentucky. It’s THC-free, finished in ex-Armagnac and Madeira casks, and a new bottling arrives each year on April 20th (which also happens to be the craft distillery’s anniversary). 

When I visited in February, I sampled the third release in the series. The aroma was predictably dank, with flavors of cedar and sandalwood incense, plus a sticky toffee pudding exhale. Yet, that viscous richness I’ve come to understand as the hallmark of cigar blends was missing. I’ll look forward to seeing how they tweak the fourth release next year.

I finally had to ask the experts: Is it crazy for a non-cigar smoker to enjoy these cigar whiskeys?

“Not at all!” Fraley exclaims. “I’ve had a lot of people tell me they don’t smoke necessarily, but they enjoy the category, which I think is pretty cool.”

Cigar Whiskeys to Try

The Dalmore Cigar Malt Reserve

Aged in a series of barrels—first American oak, then transferred to a former oloroso Sherry cask and finished in used Cabernet Sauvignon barriques—this Scotch would be an outstanding dessert accompaniment too. Look for dusty cocoa and lots of Sherried dark fruit, like figs and dates, wrapped in caramel and baking tones that linger on the spicy finish. 95 Points — K.N.

$200 Total Wine

Joseph Magnus Cigar Blend Bourbon

Concentrated vanilla and caramel aromas carry over to the complex palate, mingling with tinges of dried cherry, mocha, and clove. The long, enticing finish layers on oak and orange peel. Sip or mix into old fashioneds. Finished in Armagnac, Sherry, and Cognac casks. 96 Points — K.N.

$185 Total Wine

Barrell Craft Spirits Cigar Blend Bourbon

An excellent example of the rising crop of “cigar whiskeys” meant to pair with cigars – but truly, no cigar is required to enjoy this pour. It’s silky and light, with a relatively dry, nutty flavor that suggests walnut and chestnut, layered with char and wisps of campfire smoke. Flashes of dried fig and vanilla peek through midpalate, finishing with espresso and clove. What’s in the bottle: a blend of bourbons aged 7.5 to 18 years, finished in Madeira, Armagnac, rum, and Hungarian oak casks, and bottled at cask strength. Distilled in Kentucky, Indiana, and Tennessee. 98 Points — K.N.

$99 Total Wine

Buzzard’s Roost Double Oak Cigar Blend Bourbon

Aged in toasted and charred casks, this bourbon is designed to pair with a cigar. Oak and vanilla couples with a faint smoky waft on nose and palate. A splash of water unlocks hints of sarsaparilla and allspice, plus a brambly blackberry note. Espresso and anise mark the exhale. Distilled in Indiana. 92 Points — K.N.

$85 Buzzard’s Roost

Buzzard’s Roost Double Oak Single Barrel Cigar Rye Whiskey

Scented with savory spices and hint of mesquite, this bold whiskey opens with vanilla, almond, and torched cinnamon. Ultimately, the mix of savory and sweet reads like bacon covered with sticky maple and lots of fiery black pepper. Distilled in Indiana and finished in toasted and charred casks intended to create a smoky aroma. 91 Points — K.N.

Inquire with the distillery

The post “Midsummer Night in the Garden” A Russian River Valley Garden Party  appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Midsummer Night in the Garden

A Russian River Valley Garden Party 

Eight wines. Eight seasonal pairings. One unforgettable summer dinner party inspired by the Russian River Valley.

Long summer evenings call for wines that unfold slowly alongside the meal. Inspired by the beauty and bounty of Northern California in June, this Editor’s Table dinner party pairs J Vineyards wines with seasonal recipes designed for al fresco entertaining. Think oysters and sparkling wine at golden hour, candlelit duck confit, and a final dessert toast beneath the stars.

Built around the progression of the evening itself, the menu moves from crisp and vibrant to rich and layered, highlighting peak-season ingredients like peaches, strawberries, mushrooms, fresh seafood, and fragrant herbs. Each pairing reflects the elegance and cool-climate character of the Russian River Valley while capturing the relaxed sophistication of a midsummer garden gathering.

Aperitif – Sunlit Beginnings

J Vineyards & Winery Russian River Valley NV Cuvée 20

Passed Appetizer: Smoked Salmon Crostini

The bready, yeasty notes and fine bubbles of a Cuvée-style sparkler are a natural match for smoked salmon’s briny richness. Crisp acidity cuts through the mousse’s creamy texture while the wine’s salinity mirrors the coastal freshness of the canapé. It’s a timeless pairing that feels effortlessly elegant at golden hour.

Shop the Wine
Find Recipe

J Vineyards & Winery Russian River Valley NV Brut Rosé

Passed Appetizer: Grilled Yellow Peach Salad with Goat Cheese, Arugula & Prosciutto

Brut Rosé shines alongside stone fruit and salty cured meats, making this grilled peach salad an ideal summer pairing. Strawberry and red berry notes in the wine echo the caramelized sweetness of the peaches while vibrant acidity cuts through creamy goat cheese and rich prosciutto. Fresh arugula keeps the pairing lively, bright, and unmistakably June.

Shop the Wine
Find Recipe

First Course

2022 J Vineyards & Winery Russian River Valley Chardonnay

Perfect Pairing: Dungeness Crabmeat Ragout

This dish comes from Michelin-starred Terrapin Creek in Bodega Bay, California, where crab is sourced from docks just downhill from the restaurant. Paired with Russian River Valley Chardonnay, the combination feels indulgent and seamless. The richness of the wine mirroring the sweetness of the crab while bright acidity keeps everything lifted. It’s a beautifully California-on-California pairing that captures the spirit of coastal Northern California cuisine.

Shop the Wine
Find Recipe

2017 J Vineyards & Winery Russian River Valley Blanc de Blancs

Perfect Pairing: Oysters & Mignonette Sauce   

There may be no pairing more timeless than Blanc de Blancs and freshly shucked oysters. The wine’s bright acidity, mineral backbone, and persistent mousse mirror the oysters’ briny freshness while the sharp, vinegar-driven mignonette heightens every citrus and green apple note in the glass. Elegant and restrained, this pairing captures the cool coastal influence that defines both sparkling wine and the Russian River Valley.

Fish Course

2023 J Vineyards & Winery Russian River Valley Pinot Noir

Perfect Pairing: Sheet Pan Salmon with Pistachio Gremolata & Broccoli Rabe

Pinot Noir and salmon are a classic pairing for good reason. The wine’s bright red fruit and silky texture complement the richness of the fish without overwhelming it, while the pistachio gremolata brings fresh herbs, citrus, and crunch that amplify the wine’s earthy elegance. Broccoli rabe adds a pleasantly bitter edge that keeps the pairing vibrant and balanced, making this feel like the perfect transition from golden hour into the heart of the evening.

2023 J Vineyards & Winery Russian River Valley Bow Tie Pinot Noir

Perfect Pairing: Duck Legs Braised in Beaujolais with Prunes and Thyme

Bow Tie Pinot Noir calls for a dish with equal depth and elegance. Slow-braised duck legs layered with thyme, wine, and sweet prunes bring out the Pinot’s darker fruit character and savory complexity while echoing its silky structure. The result is rich and deeply comforting without feeling heavy. Exactly the kind of candlelit centerpiece that anchors a midsummer garden dinner party.

Perfect Pairing: Sweet & Spicy Strawberry Salad

This South Asian chaat-inspired salad layers strawberries, chickpeas, lime, herbs, and spice for a dish that feels vibrant and unexpected alongside Blanc de Noirs. The wine’s bright red fruit character and refreshing acidity play beautifully with the salad’s sweet-tart flavors while its texture softens the heat and spice. It’s a lively pairing that keeps the garden party energy going between courses.

End the evening with a dessert worthy of a mature sparkling wine. With extended lees aging, Late Disgorged sparkling develops deeper toasty complexity, layered texture, and an exceptionally fine mousse that pairs beautifully with creamy, not-too-sweet desserts. Butterscotch panna cotta with poached stone fruit, especially white peaches or nectarines, keeps the summer garden theme alive while allowing the wine’s elegance to shine through.

title: Why the World’s Most Iconic Wine Regions Are Revamping Their Classifications
summary:
Amid declining global wine consumption, even the most traditional regions are rethinking the way they classify their wines.

The post Why the World’s Most Iconic Wine Regions Are Revamping Their Classifications appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
It typically takes 10 to 15 years of research, experimentation, and aggressive bureaucratic maneuvering to make any major changes to the way wine is grown or classified across Europe. 

That’s why major recent changes in iconic regions like Bordeaux, Rioja, Germany, and Chianti Classico feel so momentous.

Bordeaux, arguably the most traditional wine region in the world, recently made headlines by introducing a new Claret classification, reviving an old style in a bid to attract young drinkers. 

“The world of wine has not seen a ton of major changes in the last 30 years,” observes Kenny Koda, wine director of The National in Telluride, Colorado. “With younger drinkers drinking less or not at all, what Bordeaux and other regions are doing makes sense. So many classification systems never really took hold here because they didn’t make a lot of sense to U.S. consumers. Making things easier to understand will help.”

Bordeaux Leans Into Freshness With Claret 

In the face of declining global wine consumption, even the most storied wine regions have needed to implement changes. 

Courting millennial drinkers has become a necessity. Drinkers in their 30s and 40s are consuming 31% of the wine sold in the U.S., according to the latest research from the Wine Market Council. They are gravitating towards fresh, fruit-forward styles, which is why Bordeaux is trying to revive an ancient form of wine: Claret. 

The name “Claret” dates back to the 12th century, but the newly approved designation, available for the first time this year from the 2025 harvest, reflects current palate preferences. 

“Younger or newer wine drinkers want clarity, freshness, and a stronger sense of place,” says Jen Saxby, senior sales and marketing manager at Napa’s Benchmark Wine Group, a leading source of rare and back-vintage wines. “Bordeaux Claret is a way to move beyond the traditional image of structured, cellar-driven reds and into a fresher, more approachable style designed to be enjoyed younger, even slightly chilled.”

Mark Johnson-Hill, CEO at Bordeaux’s Château Méaume, has mixed feelings about the shift. On the one hand, the new classification will offer a lighter side of Bordeaux—one that advocates say meets the taste and style needs of the moment.

“The ‘new’ Bordeaux Claret is lighter in body, lower in tannin, and primarily fruit-forward,” he says. “France is reclaiming a medieval word that originated in France but was repurposed by the English when they were consuming wine from Bordeaux by the barrel load.”

However, the term “Claret” can mean very different things to different members of the wine-consuming public, Johnson-Hill points out. In the United Kingdom, Claret is synonymous with dry, full-bodied red blends. “The new classification uses the same word for a fundamentally different style of wine,” he explains. “While the Bordeaux authorities acknowledge the classification is controversial, they argue they are targeting a younger audience entirely unfamiliar with the traditional connotations of the term ‘Claret,’ while helping struggling producers who currently produce Claret.”

While members of the British ruling class may be furiously fanning their blushing faces over this outrage, everyone else is pretty psyched. The change creates new opportunities for producers who need to trim their bottom lines, as well as for consumers wanting less ponderous wines.

“Producers can use higher tonnage and less winemaking to produce a lighter, friendlier Claret,” says Koda. “It’s unoaked and has 11% to 12% abv, which is cheaper to produce.”

Rioja Digs Into Authenticity

For decades, Rioja wine was classified and sold based on how long the wine aged before it was released. The longer, the better. But in recent years, the famed Spanish region has introduced several new changes to demystify the intricacies of its terroir. 

In 2017, Rioja created a way to distinguish wines made from a specific subregion, village, or vineyard. In 2024, the Consejo clarified the designation, and changed the classification of Vino de Municipio to Vino de Pueblo, putting more of a spotlight on which of Rioja’s 144 villages their wine comes from. Just as wine lovers might come to look for Napa wines first, then seek out the subregion Carneros, the thinking is that Rioja lovers may learn to gravitate more toward the Haro or Laguardia villages. 

“Interest in the specific narrative of terroir has grown in the global wine market,” says Raquel Pérez Cuevas, president of the Rioja Qualified Designation of Origin Control Board. “These changes allow Rioja to connect with consumers and speak directly to where the grapes are grown and ripened. These updates do not replace Rioja’s traditional identity, but rather enrich it.”

Javier Murúa, co-owner of Muriel Wines, agrees, adding that the era of relying on aging categories to drive demand simply isn’t enough. 

“Consumers today are far more knowledgeable and adventurous than they were in the past, and as a result, they are also less loyal to traditional categories,” Murúa says. “This has created both a consumer desire to explore and a producer need to diversify. Rioja has incredible diversity due to its size, landscapes, climates, and indigenous grape varieties. We need to do a better job of communicating why each area, village, or vineyard is distinct from another and what makes each expression unique.”

As Saxby puts it, “Today’s drinker is not just asking, ‘Is this good?’ They are asking, ‘Where is it from, why does it taste this way, and why should I care?’”

Germany Leans Into Place and Quality

The “where is it from, why does it taste this way” drumbeat is also shaping German wine’s marketing decisions.

Historically, the Verband Deutscher Prädikatsweingüter (VDP) focused exclusively on a wine’s ripeness or sweetness level. Unsurprisingly, modern consumers found this confusing, even off-putting. 

The VDP has been slowly rolling out changes to its wine-labeling system for years, with 2026 marking the first year that a revised, geography-based labeling and classification system officially became mandatory. 

The modern VDP pyramid now has four levels: Gutswein (entry-level wine), Ortswein (wines from a specific town), Erste Lage (wines from high-quality, historical vineyards), and Grosse Lage (wines from exceptional vineyards). Dry wines from the highest classification can be designated Grosses Gewächs, which is considered the most important signifier of prestige. 

“The biggest shift is toward defining wine quality through origin and site classification,” says Theresa Olkus, managing director at the VDP. “If Germany wants a classification system that is understandable and respected internationally, more precision and discipline are unavoidable. The reforms also aim to create a clearer distinction between the different quality levels, making the hierarchy easier for consumers to understand.”

Koda feels that the changes are “long overdue.”

“Germany has long fought the idea that the wines are all sweet,” he says. “This is the shot in the arm that may help Germany sell wine.”

Chianti Gets Detail-Oriented

Chianti Classico, which historically relied on its broad regional name alone to sell wines, revived the Additional Geographical Units (UGAs) system in 2023 to highlight differences within the 278-square-mile region.

“By dividing Chianti Classico into specific territories, we move from a broad, generic approach to a highly educational and terroir-driven narrative,” says Alessandro Zanette, estate manager at Vigneti La Selvanella. “It is a model that vaguely can remind one of Burgundy, which strongly resonates with those who want to understand the ‘why’ behind a wine’s profile.”

But not everyone is thrilled with the execution of the region’s rollout. 

“Unfortunately, the UGA system only applies to the loftiest, most acclaimed Chianti Gran Selezione, so it isn’t influencing the regular Classicos or Riservas,” says Richard Hanauer, wine director of RPM Restaurants, which has outlets in Chicago, D.C., and Las Vegas. “Furthermore, there are no requirements for the wineries to elevate their winemaking or vineyard selections when adding a UGA to their label.”

In the end, younger and newer wine drinkers aren’t ready to slam the door on traditional wine regions. They just want to understand what kind of experience each bottle will uncork—without enrolling in a wine education course. 

“Younger drinkers are rejecting intimidation,” Saxby argues. “If Bordeaux, Rioja, Germany, Chianti Classico, and others can translate heritage into relevance, these classification changes can become a real bridge between tradition and the next generation of wine drinkers.”

title: The Gay Bar Is Fine, Actually
summary:
When I asked around for an informal vibe check on the state of the gay bar, I expected the worst. But the sentiment was positively sunny.

The post The Gay Bar Is Fine, Actually appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
The banks aren’t tossing out rainbow pens from a float this year. This is how I typically restock my annual supply, so I’m forced to write this on a laptop. In 2015, the White House was lit up in rainbow colors. Now, a massive UFC arena—the spiritual opposite of a gay light display—has been erected on the lawn. No one drinks or has sex anymore. Support for same-sex marriage has fallen for the first time in decades. 2026 ought to be a horrendous time for the beleaguered institution of the local gay bar. But if you ask me, there’s never been a better time to enjoy a whiskey Coke poured by a bear into a plastic cup at a 5:1 whiskey-to-Coke ratio.

Throughout my long and prodigious career as a homosexual, gays have been complaining about their own bars. Complaining is a great gay artform. During the peak “corporate Pride” years, when Chipotle sold “Homo Estás” shirts, we complained it was pandering; when the sponsors fled after Trump’s reelection, we complained about that, too. Gay bars, we say, are too crowded, too expensive, too exclusionary. The music sucks. The service is awful. The only good bar in this town closed years ago. Do you remember Tic-Tac? It was perfect. It was too good for this wretched world. RIP. And so on. So when I went asking around for an informal vibe check on gay bars in our gloomy present, I expected the worst. The sentiment was positively sunny.

Why were so many gays expressing optimism. Were they sick?

“How’s the gay bar scene here?” I asked a friend while sitting at Trade in Washington, D.C.

“Honestly, great,” he said in an even, earnest tone. “They’re opening new ones,” he went on, despite the face I was making at him.

It was true in New York as well, where bars like Animal regularly see lines wrapped around the block. New York and D.C. are hardly representative of the collective, but I’d recently had the chance, for work, to travel to Denver, Minneapolis, and Houston, and found the gay bar scenes there more vivid than ever.

Vibes Shift, but the Gay Bar Endures

The dimmer the world, the brighter the bar. We trade the danger outside for a danger of our own. 

If you’ll allow a Queer Elder to wax poetic: despite alcohol allegedly being in its flop era, the gay bar has always been something apart. Like wine at Catholic mass, alcohol has an organizing power over it, but the true occasion is metaphysical. The best ones I’ve patronized have been, colloquially speaking, a hole: shabby little dinghies where you can hear the ferocious waves slapping at the sides.

Why the bar? Stonewall is the most obvious example, but the bar remains a vital organ, the health of which is a perennial matter of urgency. Are we letting too many straight people in? Should we start playing porn to keep them out? Or would that be biphobic? Misogynistic? Transphobic? I’ve attended many such symposiums while sipping nail polish remover. I can’t think of another community outside of swingers where the bar has such central importance.

Before even the word “gay,” there was “molly.” And before gay bars, there was something like them: the molly houses of 18th-century London. These were taverns, or coffeehouses, or any place where men could gather to drink and parody the straight world with, for example, mock weddings. The threat of arrest, and even death, hung over these places. It lent them their edge. 

A lot has changed since then. Gay bars are more likely these days to be called “a safe space where you can be your authentic self.” But, in keeping with its molly house roots, I find the gay bar to be something better than that. It’s a slightly dangerous space, where something honest and hungry comes out to play, and to eat. At the gay bar I enter a different self: more mischievous, more open to appetite and suggestion, more liable to leave knowing someone I didn’t know before. There’s a Dionysian aspect. Inebriation leads to revelation. Without danger, there’s no change. Without danger, there’d be no gay bar in the first place.

Change comes slowly or all at once. Tides shift, popularity waxes and wanes, and through it all the drinks have stayed stiff and we have kept complaining. What the future holds for the gay bar, like everything else in our world, is uncertain. Some, like the lesbian bar Henrietta Hudson which rebranded as a “lesbian-centric queer human bar,” have embraced the idea of becoming a “space.” Others, like the beloved Avenue Pub in Rochester did last year, are shuttering. Pride itself is in a precarious place. In the absence of corporate support, the traditional “Pride Is a Riot!” clarion call, too, feels as though it’s lacking its typical annual oomph. There’s a Girl, whatever, hanging like a pall. A sleepy Pride. A languid Pride. A great time to skip the party and hit the bar. Bartender, pour me a vodka cran!

No spoilers, but the tides will change again. Even now, Target has been successfully bullied into an anemic Pride merch collection this year, which includes among its wares a baffling trinket of two felt heterosexual birds driving a car, though one ought not, I suppose, deny the possibility of the birds being bisexual, or transgender. The point is, presidents and vibe shifts come and go. Stiff drinks and gay men complaining are forever. 

“Can we leave before the show starts?” my friend said, sitting across from me at Trade. “I know this queen. She’s not who she thinks she is.”

Ah, it feels good to be home.
